notes: The public-domain status of the medieval texts and 1868 edition does not by itself determine rights in the later encoding.

access.accessMethod: Use the complete bound scan; the target First Series is PDF pp. 5–398.
access.entityLabel: Richard Morris, Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises, First Series (1868)
access.format: 710-page PDF carrier; First Series on PDF pp. 5–398

bibliographic.citation: Richard Morris, ed., Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises (Sawles Warde, and þe Wohunge of Ure Lauerd: Ureisuns of Ure Louerd and of Ure Lefdi, &c.) of the Twelfth and Thirteenth Centuries, First Series, EETS Original Series 29 and 34 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1868), 2 vols. in 1, lxiii + 330 pp.
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.title: Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises
bibliographic.volume: First Series; EETS Original Series 29 and 34

entities.description: Morris's edited anthology of Middle English source texts with introduction, facing modern-English translations, notes, glossary, and appendices. Issued in two parts in 1867–1868 as EETS Original Series 29 and 34, it arranges the numbered texts in manuscript order by source group rather than presenting one medieval codex or one medieval work.
entities.label: Richard Morris, Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises, First Series (1868)

rights.basis: The completed First Series was published in 1868. The April 2026 U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A duration chart places works published before 1 January 1931 in the United States public domain.
rights.component: Morris's 1868 compilation, transcription, translation, notes, and apparatus

access.accessMethod: Download the complete 710-page bound scan carrier. Retain it whole; use the mapped First Series and target-work portions rather than cropping it.
access.entityLabel: Internet Archive 710-page bound scan containing Morris's First and Second Series

localCopies.label: Complete 710-page bound scan carrier containing Morris First and Second Series
notes: Coverage is complete relative to the retained 710-page PDF/carrier, not a claim that both bound series belong to the target edition.
First Series is complete on PDF pp. 5–398; Second Series preliminaries begin at PDF p. 399 and its title page is PDF p. 405.

workPortion.label: aha2688 represented matter within the First Series component
locators: First Series title leaf represented by U-M: PDF pp. 7–8
Numbered source-text pages represented by U-M: odd-numbered printed pp. 3–287 = PDF pp. 71, 73, …, 355
Appendix I pages represented by U-M: printed pp. 288–295 = PDF pp. 356–363
Appendix III page represented by U-M: printed p. 305 = PDF p. 373
notes: The odd-page progression is literal and excludes the interleaved even-numbered modern-English translations. Printed p. 200 / PDF p. 268 is an exceptional source-text page opening XXI, but aha2688 omits it; the encoded complete-Orison stream resumes at printed p. 201 / PDF p. 269.

The Appendix I wrapper in the XML begins with PB N=286, but p. 286 / PDF p. 354 contains the omitted modern-English translation of XXIX; target Appendix I content begins at p. 288 / PDF p. 356. Likewise, the Appendix III wrapper begins with PB N=304, but p. 304 / PDF p. 372 belongs to omitted Appendix II; target Appendix III begins at p. 305 / PDF p. 373.
The separately bound Second Series at PDF pp. 399–710 is present in the retained carrier but is not target matter.

access.accessMethod: Ask Lambeth Palace Library about consultation or a complete-object reproduction of MS 487; request the whole codex and map aha2688 separately to ff. 1r–67r.
access.entityLabel: London, Lambeth Palace Library MS 487
access.format: Physical consultation or commissioned images; legacy LUNA viewer currently access-gated

access.accessMethod: The legacy LUNA link redirects to a Verify Access login. Complete public extent could not be established without access.
access.entityLabel: London, Lambeth Palace Library MS 487
access.format: Legacy image viewer behind access verification

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.value: Late twelfth or early thirteenth century

entities.description: A late-twelfth- or early-thirteenth-century West Midlands parchment manuscript supplying Morris's numbered items I–XIX. The represented anthology spans ff. 1r–67r; its Poema Morale breaks off and is completed from Egerton MS 613.
entities.label: London, Lambeth Palace Library MS 487

notes: The aha2688 selection uses ff. 1r–67r.

rights.basis: The anonymous textual works and this medieval witness predate modern copyright by centuries.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval textual content represented in Lambeth MS 487
notes: This does not determine rights or contract terms for modern Lambeth photographs, scans, catalog text, or commissioned reproductions.

access.accessMethod: Use the current British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog record.
access.entityLabel: London, British Library Cotton MS Nero A XIV

access.accessMethod: The current catalog reports legacy full digital coverage, but its digital-content link is presently unavailable. Use BL consultation or digitisation enquiry and request the whole manuscript.
access.entityLabel: London, British Library Cotton MS Nero A XIV
access.format: Legacy digital coverage unavailable; physical or commissioned complete-object images may be requestable

dateStatements.label: Relevant manuscript section
dateStatements.value: Second half of the thirteenth century

entities.description: A small parchment manuscript whose second-half-thirteenth-century Middle English section includes Ancrene Wisse and the devotional pieces Morris prints as XX–XXIII. The relevant prayers occupy current ff. 120v–131r; the current catalog describes a Latin Apostles' Creed at ff. 131r–v, creating an unresolved conflict with Morris's Middle English Lesser Creed and legacy f. 131v milestone.
entities.label: London, British Library Cotton MS Nero A XIV

notes: Target material: current ff. 120v–131r, with exact terminal Creed locus unresolved.

rights.basis: The anonymous textual works and this medieval witness predate modern copyright by centuries.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval textual content represented in Cotton MS Nero A XIV
notes: This does not determine rights or contract terms for modern British Library photographs, scans, interfaces, or catalog descriptions.

access.accessMethod: Use the current British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog record.
access.entityLabel: London, British Library Cotton MS Vespasian A XXII

access.accessMethod: No current public whole-manuscript viewer was verified. Use BL consultation or digitisation enquiry and request the whole codex; map the target separately to current ff. 52r–57v.
access.entityLabel: London, British Library Cotton MS Vespasian A XXII

dateStatements.label: Composite manuscript
dateStatements.value: Relevant components span the first quarter of the thirteenth through the fourteenth century

entities.description: A composite parchment codex containing the Middle English homiletic pieces Morris prints as XXIV–XXVII. Their current catalog loci are ff. 52r–56v and 57v. Morris's legacy labels are consistently two folios higher: legacy ff. 54r–59v = current ff. 52r–57v; current = legacy − 2.
entities.label: London, British Library Cotton MS Vespasian A XXII

notes: The relevant pieces are cataloged at current ff. 52r–56v and 57v; the component has a Rochester Cathedral Priory provenance association.

rights.basis: The anonymous textual works and this medieval witness predate modern copyright by centuries.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval textual content represented in Cotton MS Vespasian A XXII
notes: This does not determine rights or contract terms for modern British Library photographs, scans, interfaces, or catalog descriptions.

access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Bodley 34

access.accessMethod: Use the Bodleian special-collections copying route and request the whole current object, including covers, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, and edges where available; map Sawles Warde separately to ff. 72r–80v.
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Bodley 34
access.format: Commissioned complete-object images or mediated copies

access.accessMethod: No current public complete Digital Bodleian object was verified. A 1960 EETS printed facsimile is cataloged but does not establish a reusable public whole-codex digital copy.
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Bodley 34
access.format: No verified public complete digital facsimile

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.value: First half of the thirteenth century, perhaps c. 1240

entities.description: A first-half-thirteenth-century parchment manuscript, perhaps copied around 1240 in the southwest Midlands or Herefordshire, containing Sawles Warde at current ff. 72r–80v. Two leaves are missing after f. 80, so Morris supplies the ending from Royal MS 17 A XXVII. Morris's legacy locus ff. 76v–84v corresponds to the current catalog's ff. 72r–80v.
entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Bodley 34

notes: Sawles Warde occupies current ff. 72r–80v; two leaves are missing after f. 80.

rights.basis: The anonymous textual works and this medieval witness predate modern copyright by centuries.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval textual content represented in Bodleian MS Bodley 34
notes: This does not determine rights or contract terms for modern Bodleian photographs, scans, catalog text, or commissioned reproductions.

access.accessMethod: Use the component and parent British Library catalog records.
access.entityLabel: London, British Library Royal MS 17 A XXVII

access.accessMethod: Legacy cataloging reported full digital coverage, but no current complete public inventory was available. Use BL consultation or digitisation enquiry and request the whole manuscript.
access.entityLabel: London, British Library Royal MS 17 A XXVII
access.format: Legacy digital coverage unavailable; complete-object images may be requestable

dateStatements.label: Relevant manuscript sections
dateStatements.value: First half of the thirteenth century

entities.description: A first-half-thirteenth-century central or West Midlands parchment manuscript containing Sawles Warde at ff. 1r–10v and an Orison of St Mary at f. 70r–v. Its Sawles Warde text supplies Morris's missing Bodley ending, carries the Johan scribal colophon and nine-line prayer at f. 10v, and serves as a collation witness throughout.
entities.label: London, British Library Royal MS 17 A XXVII

notes: Target loci: Sawles Warde ff. 1r–10v; Orison of St Mary f. 70r–v.

rights.basis: The anonymous textual works, colophon, prayer, and medieval witness predate modern copyright by centuries.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval textual content and witness paratext represented in Royal MS 17 A XXVII
notes: This does not determine rights or contract terms for modern British Library photographs, scans, interfaces, or catalog descriptions.

access.accessMethod: Use the current British Library component catalog record.
access.entityLabel: London, British Library Cotton MS Titus D XVIII

access.accessMethod: The formerly linked full digital object is not currently available through the catalog. Use BL consultation or digitisation enquiry and request the whole manuscript.
access.entityLabel: London, British Library Cotton MS Titus D XVIII

dateStatements.label: Katherine Group component
dateStatements.value: First half of the thirteenth century

entities.description: A first-half-thirteenth-century parchment manuscript whose Katherine Group component includes Sawles Warde at ff. 105v–112v, Hali Meiðhad at ff. 112v–127r, Þe Wohunge of Ure Lauerd at ff. 127r–133r, and Seinte Katerine at ff. 133v–147v. Morris transcribes the Wohunge and records selected Titus readings for Sawles Warde.
entities.label: London, British Library Cotton MS Titus D XVIII

notes: The relevant component is written in two columns; Þe Wohunge occupies ff. 127r–133r.

rights.basis: The anonymous textual works and this medieval witness predate modern copyright by centuries.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval textual content represented in Cotton MS Titus D XVIII
notes: This does not determine rights or contract terms for modern British Library photographs, scans, interfaces, or catalog descriptions.

access.accessMethod: Use the current British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog record.
access.entityLabel: London, British Library Egerton MS 613

entities.description: A composite parchment miscellany containing the first Egerton Poema Morale copy at ff. 7r–12v. Morris uses it to continue the incomplete Lambeth version in XVIII and prints the Egerton witness in Appendix I.
entities.label: London, British Library Egerton MS 613

physicalDescription.extent: 74 folios plus ten modern paper endleaves at each end
notes: Target copy at ff. 7r–12v; complete digital object has 197 canvases from front cover through spine.

rights.basis: The anonymous textual works and this medieval witness predate modern copyright by centuries.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval textual content represented in Egerton MS 613
notes: This does not determine rights or contract terms for the modern IIIF photographs.

notes: Canvas 1 is the front cover; canvas 197 is the spine; target ff. 7r–12v are canvases 39–50.

access.accessMethod: No surviving object, repository, catalog record, or reproduction route has been identified. Trace Morris and Parker papers before requesting a copy.
access.entityLabel: George Parker's working transcript and collations for Morris's Sawles Warde

dateStatements.label: Preparation
dateStatements.value: Before publication of the First Series in 1867–1868

entities.description: A provisional entity for the nineteenth-century working transcript and collations that Morris explicitly credits to George Parker. It is modern editorial working material, not a medieval witness. Its survival, present location, physical extent, and exact handoff into the printed edition remain unknown.
entities.label: George Parker's working transcript and collations for Morris's Sawles Warde
notes: Typed as manuscript_witness only because the schema has no separate editorial-working-document class; the description and relations keep its modern status explicit.

rights.basis: The working artifact's present survival, repository, publication history, and exact authorship details are not established.
rights.component: George Parker's nineteenth-century transcript and collations, if extant

access.accessMethod: Download the official four-page PDF directly from the U.S. Copyright Office. This is supporting rights guidance, not a corpus text source.
access.entityLabel: U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A

localCopies.label: U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A, April 2026
notes: Rights-research guidance only: this PDF contains no aha2688 text, images, edition pages, or manuscript material.
The exact official provider bytes are preserved unchanged. pdfinfo reads all four pages; qpdf completes successfully with known linearization hint-table warnings.

rights.basis: The circular is an official United States Government work; 17 U.S.C. 105 makes United States copyright protection unavailable for such works, subject to the section's exceptions.
rights.component: Official U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A PDF

entities.description: April 2026 duration guidance used only for the United States rights assessment of Morris's nineteenth-century publication; it is supporting legal evidence, contains no aha2688 target content, and is not part of the textual lineage.
entities.label: U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A

rights.basis: The circular is an official United States Government work; 17 U.S.C. 105 makes United States copyright protection unavailable for such works, subject to the section's exceptions.

next_steps: Use the repository-specific consultation and reproduction routes recorded in access.
Request each entire current physical object rather than target folios alone, then map aha2688 portions inside the complete delivery.
Record provider terms, shot sequence, omissions, and exact file hashes before creating any local cache bundle.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, current, reusable whole-object facsimiles be obtained for Lambeth 487, Nero A XIV, Vespasian A XXII, Bodley 34, Royal 17 A XXVII, and Titus D XVIII?

next_steps: Inspect complete current images of Nero A XIV ff. 131r–v.
Compare the manuscript directly with Morris pp. 215–217 and the XML DIV3 creed.
Preserve any foliation, language, or item-boundary correction as an explicit crosswalk rather than replacing the legacy claim silently.
openQuestions.question: How does Morris's Middle English Þe Lesse Crede and legacy f. 131v milestone correspond to the current BL catalog's Latin Apostles' Creed at ff. 131r–v?

next_steps: Search Morris, Parker, EETS, and Oxford archival papers for the credited transcript and collations.
If located, create a separate catalog description and reproduction request rather than collapsing it into a direct Morris-to-Bodley edge.
Compare its readings with Morris's print to document the exact editorial handoff.
openQuestions.question: Does George Parker's working Bodley transcript and Royal/Titus collation survive, and where is it held?

next_steps: Ask the British Library to clarify UK image status, systematic IIIF extraction, local research retention, and repository redistribution.
Use the public 197-canvas viewer for inspection meanwhile, without bulk-caching it.
Record any written permission and its exact geographic, commercial, attribution, and redistribution scope.
openQuestions.question: What permission or provider-approved extraction route applies to downloading and redistributing a complete Egerton MS 613 image bundle, especially in the United Kingdom?

next_steps: Inspect any surviving U-M page-image inventory and OCR production logs for missing image 00000264.tif or selection rules.
Compare U-M's current downloadable source, if available, with the checked-in XML.
Keep the print edition's complete XXI separate from the digital stream's partial representation.
openQuestions.question: Why does the U-M encoding omit Morris p. 200 / Nero f. 124r and the XXI heading while retaining pp. 201–203?

relations.objectLabel: Richard Morris, Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises, First Series (1868)

scope.notes: The encoding omits the half title and front matter, all facing even-numbered modern-English translation pages, Appendix II, glossary/index/back matter, and much of the printed apparatus. Printed p. 200 / PDF p. 268 is an exceptional source-text page rather than a translation page, but it too is omitted. Printed XX and XXI are merged into XML DIV2 N=20; for XXI the subject stream begins only at Morris p. 201 / PDF p. 269 / Nero f. 124v and omits the heading and opening at p. 200 / f. 124r.

object.description: First Series title page and selected source-text pages
locators: numbered texts I–XXIX on odd-numbered printed pp. 3–287 / PDF pp. 71, 73, …, 355
Appendix I on printed pp. 288–295 / PDF pp. 356–363
Appendix III on printed p. 305 / PDF p. 373

reviewNotes: Physical manuscripts, medieval textual works, nineteenth-century editorial products, digital facsimiles, U-M encoding, and repository artifact are modeled as separate layers.
Morris legacy foliation for Cotton Vespasian A XXII is two folios higher than the current BL foliation: Morris legacy ff. 54r–59v = current BL ff. 52r–57v; current = legacy − 2.
No current manuscript image was inferred from a legacy catalog statement or inaccessible viewer. Whole-object facsimile extent is distinguished from work presence and exact work loci.

The ignored cache contains five verified files: the 710-page Morris bound carrier with aha2688 present; target-absent Internet Archive item-metadata, printed-page-map, and scan-data sidecars; and the target-absent four-page USCO Circular 15A. The circular supports rights analysis only and does not alter the substantive work chain.

The Appendix I wrapper in the XML begins with PB N=286, but p. 286 / PDF p. 354 contains the omitted modern-English translation of XXIX; target Appendix I content begins at p. 288 / PDF p. 356. Likewise, the Appendix III wrapper begins with PB N=304, but p. 304 / PDF p. 372 belongs to omitted Appendix II; target Appendix III begins at p. 305 / PDF p. 373.
The separately bound Second Series at PDF pp. 399–710 is present in the retained carrier but is not target matter.

lineage.summary: aha2688 is the repository copy of the University of Michigan OCR/Level-1 TEI representation of selected Middle English source texts from Richard Morris's Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises, First Series (EETS Original Series 29 and 34, completed 1868). Morris compiled twenty-nine numbered print containers from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487, Cotton MSS Nero A XIV and Vespasian A XXII, Bodleian MS Bodley 34, Royal MS 17 A XXVII, Cotton MS Titus D XVIII, and Egerton MS 613. George Parker supplied the Bodley transcript and Royal/Titus collations behind Morris's Sawles Warde text. The U-M encoding omits Morris's introduction, facing modern-English translations, much apparatus, Appendix II, and back matter; it includes Appendix I and Appendix III. It also merges printed XX and XXI into XML division N=20 and represents the complete Nero Orison only partially, beginning at Morris p. 201 / Nero f. 124v after omitting its heading and p. 200 / f. 124r opening. The retained 710-page Internet Archive PDF is complete as a scan carrier, but only PDF pp. 5–398 reproduce the target First Series; a separately bound Second Series begins at PDF p. 399 and is not part of the target edition. Exact Internet Archive item-metadata, printed-page-map, and scan-data sidecars are retained separately as target-absent supporting files. Egerton MS 613 has a complete 197-canvas public IIIF object, mapped to the target at ff. 7r–12v / canvases 39–50, but it is not bulk-cached because the manifest limits its public-domain statement geographically and BL terms require caution about systematic extraction. No complete public facsimile was verified for the other six manuscripts. An exact official April 2026 U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A PDF is cached separately as supporting United States rights evidence; it contains no aha2688 text or images and is not part of the substantive transmission chain.

catalogSummary.title: Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises, First Series

catalogingSubject.label: Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises, First Series
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The edition-defined selection of Middle English source texts represented by the U-M item aha2688 from Richard Morris's First Series: twenty-nine numbered print containers, with the complete Nero version of the Orison of God treated as a witness version of the incomplete Lambeth item rather than a second abstract work, and with the separately headed Lesser Creed counted as an additional intellectual work. The selected appendices preserve parallel witness versions, not new works. This cataloging unit is not a single medieval work, one manuscript, the complete 1868 publication, its omitted modern-English translations and apparatus, the U-M encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact.

assertion.notes: The component works and surviving copies have distinct dates; witness production dates are recorded in lineage and are not automatically work-composition dates.

scope.description: Broad date range for the component Middle English works and versions, not one composition event

extent.description: The cataloged selection has twenty-nine numbered print containers but twenty-nine intellectual works only after version and nesting boundaries are applied. Printed XXI is a complete witness version of XIX and contributes zero new works; the separately headed Lesser Creed nested under XXIII contributes one. The intrinsic verse totals are 305 lines in seven groups for VI, 396 lines in one group for XVIII, and 171 lines in one group for XX. Raw XML totals are eleven LG and 1,153 L because they also count the 272-line Egerton parallel Moral Ode and the nine-line Royal witness prayer after Sawles Warde. The thirty-three modeled sections are the twenty-nine numbered containers, the Lesser Creed, the Royal witness paratext, and the two represented appendix versions.

formStatements.description: Twenty-six represented intellectual works are prose-only and three are verse-only. Parallel appendix versions and witness-specific paratext are excluded from the intellectual-work form totals.

scope.description: The dominant genre of the anthology; not every numbered prayer or poem is itself a homily
The selected vernacular homilies, prayers, poems, and devotional prose
Biblical and liturgical lemmata, quotations, formulae, and rubrics embedded within the Middle English works

notes: The record preserves three separate counts: twenty-nine numbered print containers, twenty-eight encoded DIV2 elements, and twenty-nine intellectual works.

Printed XXI is the complete Nero witness version corresponding to the incomplete Lambeth Orison printed as XIX. Its U-M stream is partial because the XML begins at Morris p. 201 / Nero f. 124v and omits p. 200 / f. 124r and the heading.
Morris's title term Old English is retained as bibliographic evidence; Early Middle English is the descriptive language category used for the vernacular works.

Modern-English translations, introduction, much apparatus, Appendix II, and back matter remain outside the represented text selection even though the complete First Series scan is available in lineage.

next_steps: Review modern specialist editions and repertories component by component.
Preserve witness-specific versions and explicit uncertainty rather than assigning one date, author, or dialect to the anthology.
openQuestions.question: Which modern work records, dates, localizations, and source identifications should replace or qualify Morris's nineteenth-century descriptions for every anonymous component?

next_steps: Obtain U-M production documentation beyond the inherited XML header.
Compare any current downloadable U-M XML bitstream with the checked-in artifact.
openQuestions.question: What keying, OCR, image-link, and revision history lies between the 1868 copy and U-M's 2006 Level-1 TEI representation?

assertion.notes: The witnesses have different production localizations; this broad work-level statement does not collapse those physical contexts into one composition site.

scope.description: Broad cultural origin of the component Middle English works

assertion.notes: The anthology contains multiple anonymous works. Shared dialect, manuscript context, or traditional grouping does not establish one author for the whole selection.

scope.description: The medieval works and versions assembled in Morris's selection
Selection and arrangement of the numbered texts, translations, notes, and appendices
The printed Middle English text, with witness-specific dependencies and George Parker's working contribution recorded in lineage
The modern-English translations in the 1868 print edition, which the U-M XML explicitly omits

assertion.notes: This responsibility is routed through a provisional historical working-transcript entity in lineage; it does not imply that the checked-in XML was transcribed directly by Parker.

scope.description: The Bodley-based Sawles Warde working transcript supplied to Morris, including the Royal terminal supply
Royal marginal readings and selected Cotton Titus D XVIII readings supplied for Morris's Sawles Warde text and notes

subjects.label: Prayer and devotion to Christ and the Virgin Mary
The soul, sin, judgment, and salvation

summaries.value: Richard Morris's First Series brings together anonymous Early Middle English homilies, doctrinal expositions, prayers, devotional prose, and religious poems from seven principal manuscript witnesses. Its subjects include the liturgical year, penitence, the Creed and Pater Noster, moral conduct, death and judgment, devotion to Christ and Mary, guardianship of the soul, and the wooing of Christ. The U-M representation preserves the Middle English source-text stream but not the facing modern-English translations.

Seventeen numbered Lambeth homiletic, doctrinal, and verse pieces; the Lambeth and Egerton versions of Poema Morale; an incomplete Lambeth and a complete Nero version of an Orison of God; a Marian poem; prayers and praises from Nero; four Vespasian homiletic pieces; Sawles Warde from Bodley with Royal supply and collation; þe Wohunge of Ure Lauerd from Titus; and selected parallel-text appendices from Egerton and Royal.

The cataloging unit follows the represented source-text selection. It separates twenty-nine print containers, twenty-eight XML DIV2 elements, and twenty-nine intellectual works: XML division N=20 merges printed XX and XXI; XXI is a complete witness version of the incomplete work printed as XIX and therefore adds no new abstract work; the separately headed Lesser Creed within XXIII does add one. Appendix I and Appendix III are parallel witness versions, and the Royal colophon and nine-line prayer after Sawles Warde are witness paratext.

assertion.notes: Morris's title uses Old English in nineteenth-century usage; the represented vernacular texts are cataloged here as Early Middle English.

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus cataloging from Morris's title page and series statement

Its subjects include the liturgical year, penitence, the Creed and Pater Noster, moral conduct, death and judgment, devotion to Christ and Mary, guardianship of the soul, and the wooing of Christ.
